title: Hot Work Permit Requirements — Gas Testing and LEL Thresholds
content: OISD Standard 116, Section 5.3 — Hot Work in Hazardous Areas: Hot work (welding, cutting, grinding, use of open flame) shall not be permitted in any area where the concentration of flammable gas or vapour exceeds 20% of the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL). Before issuing a Hot Work Permit, a competent person shall test the atmosphere using an approved combustible gas detector. Testing shall be conducted at the exact location of proposed hot work and at all adjacent locations within a 10-metre radius. If LEL reading exceeds 10%, additional precautions must be documented. If LEL exceeds 20%, the permit shall be withheld until the source is identified and atmosphere is confirmed below threshold by two successive tests 30 minutes apart. Hot work permits shall specify the maximum permissible LEL at the work location and require re-testing every 30 minutes during work execution. Any increase beyond 20% LEL during work shall result in immediate suspension of hot work and re-assessment of area conditions. The permit-issuing authority must physically inspect the work area before signature and shall not issue permits based solely on verbal reports or previous test results from prior shifts.

title: Confined Space Entry — Pre-Entry Atmospheric Testing Requirements
content: OISD Standard 116, Section 8.1 — Confined Space Entry Procedures: No person shall enter a confined space unless a valid Confined Space Entry Permit has been issued and the following atmospheric conditions have been verified by a competent person within 30 minutes prior to entry: (a) Oxygen content between 19.5% and 23.5% by volume; (b) Flammable gas/vapour concentration below 10% LEL; (c) Toxic gas concentrations below applicable OEL/TLV values (CO below 25 ppm, H2S below 5 ppm, SO2 below 0.25 ppm). Testing must be performed at multiple points within the confined space — at the entry point, at the lowest accessible point, and at the work location. A single entry-point reading is insufficient. Results shall be recorded on the permit form with the tester's signature and time. Continuous atmospheric monitoring shall be maintained during confined space occupancy using calibrated instruments. If continuous monitoring is not available, periodic testing at intervals not exceeding 30 minutes is mandatory. Entry shall be immediately suspended and occupants evacuated if any parameter exceeds the above limits during work.

title: Simultaneous Operations — Multi-Permit Hazard Assessment
content: OISD Standard 116, Section 6.4 — Simultaneous Operations (SIMOPS) Control: When two or more work permits are active simultaneously in the same area or adjacent areas, a combined hazard assessment (Simultaneous Operations Assessment) must be conducted before any individual permit becomes active. The site safety officer or permit authority must evaluate the combined effect of all simultaneous activities on the overall risk level of the area. Activities that individually present acceptable risk may, in combination, create unacceptable compound hazards. Examples requiring mandatory SIMOPS review: (a) Hot work and any activity involving opening of process equipment in adjacent zones; (b) Confined space entry and pressure testing in adjoining pipework or vessels; (c) Electrical isolation work and any equipment start-up operations nearby; (d) Any simultaneous activity in zones where gas monitoring coverage is degraded or equipment is offline. A single permit authority shall be designated as SIMOPS coordinator when multiple permits are active. The SIMOPS coordinator must be notified before any new permit is issued in an area with existing active permits. Where SIMOPS assessment identifies elevated combined risk, additional controls shall be mandatory including increased monitoring frequency, exclusion zones, and enhanced communication between work parties.

title: Gas Detection System — Backup Requirements During Maintenance
content: OISD Standard 116, Section 9.2 — Continuity of Gas Detection Coverage: Fixed gas detection systems are safety-critical instruments. When any fixed gas detector is taken offline for maintenance, calibration, or replacement, the following compensatory measures shall be implemented without exception: (a) The area covered by the offline detector shall be declared a temporary restricted zone; (b) Any active work permits in the coverage zone of the offline detector shall be suspended pending risk reassessment; (c) Portable gas detectors with continuous monitoring capability shall be deployed as temporary replacements before any permit in the affected zone is reinstated; (d) Increased manual gas testing at 15-minute intervals shall be conducted in the affected zone by a competent safety technician; (e) The maintenance planner shall coordinate gas detector maintenance schedules with the permit authority to avoid simultaneous detector outages in adjacent zones. No work permit shall be issued in a zone where the fixed gas detection system is offline without documented compensatory monitoring controls. The permit-to-work system shall flag active permits in zones where detector status changes to offline.

title: Permit to Work — General Requirements and Shift Handover
content: OISD Standard 116, Section 5.1 — Permit to Work System General Requirements: A formal written Permit to Work (PTW) system is mandatory for all non-routine work activities in process areas, hazardous areas, and confined spaces in industrial facilities. The PTW system must cover the following work categories at minimum: Hot Work, Confined Space Entry, Electrical Isolation, Cold Work, Excavation, and Work at Height in hazardous areas. Each permit must specify: the exact work location including zone identifier and equipment tag numbers; work description and scope; duration of validity (maximum 12 hours per shift, permits do not auto-extend across shift changeovers); identified hazards and risk assessment findings; required gas testing results with times; PPE requirements; emergency procedures specific to the work; names of all persons authorised to work under the permit; and signature of issuing authority. Shift Handover: All active permits must be formally handed over at shift change. The incoming shift supervisor must physically inspect the work site, verify current atmospheric conditions, and re-sign the permit or issue a new permit as appropriate. Permits shall not be assumed to remain valid across shift changes without this verification. Emergency Suspension: Any permit authority or site safety officer has the authority to immediately suspend any active permit if site conditions change materially from those at the time of issuance.

title: Confined Space Entry — Standby Person and Rescue Requirements
content: OISD Standard 116, Section 8.5 — Standby Person Requirements for Confined Space Entry: A trained standby person shall be stationed at the entry point of every confined space from the moment of first entry until the last person exits. The standby person's sole duty during confined space occupancy is to maintain communication with workers inside, monitor atmospheric conditions at the entry point, and initiate rescue procedures if an emergency occurs. The standby person shall not enter the confined space under any circumstances unless they are a trained rescue team member equipped with appropriate SCBA and a second standby person is immediately available outside. The standby person must maintain a log of all persons entering and exiting the confined space with entry and exit times. Communication with workers inside shall be maintained at intervals not exceeding 5 minutes. If contact is lost for more than 5 minutes, the standby person shall immediately initiate the confined space emergency response procedure. Responsibilities of the standby person: continuous monitoring of entrant status and atmospheric readings; maintaining the entry log; controlling access to the confined space by unauthorised persons; alerting emergency response when required; initiating rescue procedures according to the site emergency plan. The standby person must be trained in confined space rescue procedures and in the use of rescue equipment including tripod, lifeline, and SCBA.

title: Atmospheric Testing — Multiple Sampling Points in Large Spaces
content: OISD Standard 105, Section 5.2 — Atmospheric Sampling Methodology: The atmosphere in confined spaces, tanks, vessels, and enclosed process areas must be sampled at multiple representative locations to ensure uniform coverage. For spaces larger than 50 cubic metres, sampling at the entry point only is explicitly prohibited. Required sampling points include: (a) The entry/exit point at mid-height; (b) The lowest accessible point (gases denser than air — hydrocarbons, H2S — accumulate here); (c) The highest accessible point (gases lighter than air — methane, hydrogen — accumulate here); (d) All corners and dead-end sections where stratification may occur; (e) The actual location of proposed work activity. Sampling instruments shall be inserted into the space using a sampling probe of adequate length — do not rely on readings at the opening when the probe cannot reach the work area. For confined spaces requiring entry, all sampling must be conducted before entry. Remote sampling methods should be used whenever feasible. If any single sample point shows concentrations above threshold values, the entire space shall be treated as unsafe until the source is identified and remediated. A gas-free certificate is valid only when all required sampling points show safe readings.

title: Petroleum Refinery Safety — Continuous Atmospheric Monitoring in Process Areas
content: OISD Standard 118, Section 7.2 — Continuous Monitoring Requirements for Petroleum Refineries: All classified hazardous areas in petroleum refineries (as defined by the Petroleum Rules and IS 5572) shall be equipped with fixed continuous atmospheric monitoring systems for hydrocarbons (LEL-based) and toxic gases (H2S minimum, CO where applicable). Fixed detectors shall be installed at: (a) All potential leak sources including flanges, pump seals, and valve packing glands in hydrocarbon service; (b) Low-lying areas where dense vapours can accumulate (drains, pits, sumps); (c) Compressor houses and pump houses; (d) All confined spaces with permanent or semi-permanent process connections. Alarm set points shall be: First alarm (warning) at 10% LEL or 50% of toxic gas TLV-TWA; Second alarm (evacuation) at 25% LEL or 100% of toxic gas TLV-TWA. Detectors must be maintained in accordance with manufacturer specifications. Calibration records shall be maintained and available for inspection. All detector alarms must be reported to a continuously manned control room. The control room must have documented procedures for each alarm condition including verification steps and escalation protocols. Integration with the Permit to Work system is required — the control room shall have authority to suspend permits in areas where detectors alarm.

title: Precautions Against Dangerous Fumes and Gases
content: Factories Act 1948, Section 36 — Precautions in case of fire: (1) In every factory all practicable measures shall be taken to prevent outbreak of fire and its spread both internally and externally. (2) Safe means of escape shall be provided for all persons in the event of a fire. (3) The necessary equipment and facilities for extinguishing fire shall be provided and maintained. (4) All workers shall be familiar with the means of escape and drilled in their use. Regarding Precautions Against Dangerous Fumes — Section 36A: No person shall be required or allowed to enter any chamber, tank, vat, pit, pipe, flue or other confined space in any factory in which any gas, fume, vapour or dust is likely to be present to such extent as to involve risk to persons being overcome thereby, unless it is provided with a manhole of adequate size or other effective means of egress. If any such confined space is entered by any person for the purpose of work therein, suitable respiratoryprotective equipment shall be provided free of charge to and used by the person entering. A person trained in rescue operations shall be stationed outside the confined space. All necessary precautions shall be taken to prevent dangerous accumulation of any gas, fume, vapour or dust in any workroom or other part of the factory.

title: Explosive or Inflammable Dust, Gas, Fume or Vapour
content: Factories Act 1948, Section 38 — Explosive or inflammable dust, gas, fume or vapour: (1) Where in any factory any manufacturing process produces dust, gas, fume or vapour of such character and to such extent as to be likely to explode on ignition, all practicable measures shall be taken to prevent any such explosion by: (a) effective enclosure of the plant or machinery used in the process; (b) removal or prevention of the accumulation of such dust, gas, fume or vapour; (c) exclusion or effective enclosure of all possible sources of ignition. (2) Where in any factory the plant or machinery used in a process such as is referred to in sub-section (1) is not so constructed as to withstand the probable pressure which such an explosion as aforesaid would produce, all practicable measures shall be taken to restrict the spread and effects of the explosion by the use of chokes, baffles, vents or other effective appliances. (3) Where any part of a plant or machinery in a factory contains any explosive or inflammable gas or vapour under pressure greater than atmospheric pressure, that part shall not be opened except in accordance with the following provisions, namely: (a) before the fastening of any joint of any pipe connected with the part or the fastening of the cover of any opening into the part is loosened, any flow of the gas or vapour into the part or into any such pipe shall be effectively stopped by a stop-valve or other means; (b) before any such fastening is removed, all practicable measures shall be taken to reduce the pressure of the gas or vapour in the part or pipe to atmospheric pressure.

title: Emergency Response — Incident Classification and Escalation
content: OISD Standard 116, Section 7.1 — Emergency Response Classification: Industrial incidents shall be classified into three levels to enable proportionate response: Level 1 (On-site Emergency): Incident confined to a single equipment or process unit, manageable by plant emergency team without external resources. Triggers: single gas detector alarm above 25% LEL with source identified; small fire extinguished within 60 seconds; minor chemical spill contained to process area. Response: plant emergency team, evacuation of immediate area, notification of site safety officer. Level 2 (Major Emergency): Incident involving multiple units or zones, requiring mobilisation of on-site emergency brigade and potentially requesting mutual aid. Triggers: compound sensor alarms across multiple zones; uncontrolled fire; gas release with multiple detector alarms; any confirmed fatality or serious injury. Response: full plant evacuation of affected zones, emergency brigade deployment, notification of District Emergency Authority and DGFASLI, police and fire services. Level 3 (Catastrophic Emergency): Incident with potential off-site impact requiring external emergency services and government notification. Response: complete plant evacuation, external emergency services, DGFASLI immediate notification (within 4 hours), Ministry of Labour notification. All Level 2 and Level 3 incidents require preliminary regulatory report submission to DGFASLI within 24 hours of incident using Form 18 (Fatal Accident Report) or Form 19 (Dangerous Occurrence Report) under the Factories Act 1948.

title: Mandatory Personal Gas Monitors in Hazardous Gas Zones
content: Directorate General of Mines Safety Circular No. 11 of 2020 — Mandatory Use of Personal Gas Detectors: This circular applies to all mines and metalliferous factories where workers are exposed to potential risk from flammable or toxic gases. With effect from January 1, 2021, personal gas detection devices are mandatory for all workers entering the following areas: (a) Underground workings where methane or other flammable gases may be present; (b) Any surface area classified as Zone 1 or Zone 2 under the Petroleum Rules or Explosives Act hazardous area classification; (c) Blast furnace areas, coke oven areas, and any area where BFG (Blast Furnace Gas), COG (Coke Oven Gas), or BOFG (Basic Oxygen Furnace Gas) is present or may be present; (d) Any confined space where toxic gas exposure is a recognised hazard. Personal gas detectors must be calibrated at least monthly and must alarm at: CO — 25 ppm; H2S — 5 ppm; O2 deficiency below 19.5%; O2 enrichment above 23.5%; Flammable gases — 10% LEL. Workers shall not enter designated hazardous areas without a functional personal gas detector. Supervisors are responsible for verifying detector functionality before shift commencement. Work permits for activities in hazardous gas zones shall specify personal gas detector requirements as a permit condition.
